title: Strongyloidiasis (Archived) -- Evaluation
content: High serum IgE levels are present in 39% to 58% of the cases. This association between elevated serum IgE levels and strongyloidiasis is, however, rarely present in  HTLV-1 co-infected individuals, indicating an inability of HTLV-I-positive patients to clear S. stercoralis.

[14] The microscopic examination of stool for larval forms still remains the gold standard of diagnosis. However, a single stool exam has only about 50% sensitivity.

title: Strongyloidiasis (Archived) -- Evaluation
content: Diagnostic sensitivity is enhanced by combining stool testing with serologic testing by Enzyme-linked Immunosorbent assay( ELISA). ELISA  has a high negative predictive value and can thus help in ruling out infection, particularly in patients to be subjected to immunosuppressive treatment. However, it identifies IgG and thus does not distinguish between current and prior infection.

title: Strongyloidiasis -- Etiology
content: Host factors such as HIV infection, human T-lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1) infection, and alcoholism have been reported as risk factors for Strongyloidiasis. The most prominent risk factors for severe disease include immunosuppressive therapies (including corticosteroid use), diseases like diabetes, HIV, HTLV infection, and alcoholism. [7] Poor sanitation of households and walking barefoot have been postulated to play an important role in the etiology of this infection as well. [7] Contrary to popular belief, HIV does not seem to be a risk factor for superinfection. This is likely secondary to HIV-induced CD4 cell immunosuppression predisposing more to bacterial and viral infections than parasitic infections. [8]

content: The prognosis in strongyloidiasis depends on the development of complications. Strongyloidiasis can result in dermatologic, gastrointestinal, renal, pulmonary, and neurologic complications and even death.

title: Tapeworm -- Differential Diagnosis
content: Given the asymptomatic or mild presentation and a wide range of symptoms for adult tapeworm infections, the diagnosis requires high clinical suspicion based on the patient's travel or exposure history of endemic regions. Patients in endemic regions who present with abdominal pain, diarrhea, or constipation merit stool microscopy. Given the low sensitivity of stool microscopy, negative results should not eliminate tapeworm infection from the differential; however, a broad differential for abdominal pain should be maintained to include other infectious etiologies (bacterial diarrhea, viral syndromes) or chronic inflammatory disorders. Repeat testing or empiric treatment may be necessary.

title: Viral Diarrhea -- Differential Diagnosis
content: The differential diagnosis for acute viral gastroenteritis encompasses other infectious origins, including viral, bacterial, and parasitic infections, as well as noninfectious factors that contribute to acute diarrhea. When dealing with cases of diarrhea persisting for over 1 week, it is essential to gather a comprehensive patient history, including inquiries about recent travel, hiking, or engagement in oral or anal sexual activity. A positive historical account should lead to evaluation for protozoa such as Cryptosporidium and Giardia . In recent hospitalization or antibiotic use, it is essential to consider the possibility of C difficile infection.

title: Strongyloidiasis -- History and Physical -- Acute Infection
content: Larva currens or “running” larva is a pathognomonic dermatologic manifestation of strongyloidiasis and can be seen with autoinfection as well. Larva currens manifest as a raised, pink, pruritic, serpiginous streak and can progress approximately 5 to 15 cm per hour. [17]

title: Amebiasis(Archived) -- Introduction
content: If the parasite reaches the bloodstream, it can spread through the body and end up in the liver, causing amoebic liver abscesses. Liver abscesses can occur without previous diarrhea. Diagnosis is typically by stool examination using a microscope. An increased WBC count may be present. The most accurate test is specific antibodies in the blood.

title: Hookworm -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: The diagnosis and management of hookworm infections necessitate an interprofessional approach, including histopathologist, infectious disease specialist, gastroenterologist, and general practitioner. Patients usually present to their general practitioner with general non-specific symptoms or anemia. Diagnosis requires a high index of suspicion with careful history and examination, especially in non-endemic areas, besides a thorough analysis of stool samples from experienced histopathologists. Referrals might be necessary for both infectious disease specialists or gastroenterologists, and both have to be mindful of such a diagnosis for suggestive presentations. Treatment requires collaboration between pharmacists, nurses, and physicians.

title: Strongyloidiasis -- Evaluation
content: Peripheral eosinophilia is a common finding in patients with chronic infections. [27] However, the sensitivity of eosinophilia for strongyloidiasis is low. Moreover, in patients with hyperinfection syndrome, peripheral eosinophilia is usually absent. [28] The diagnosis of chronic strongyloidiasis is especially challenging due to low parasitic load, which results in low sensitivity of stool microscopy and low specificity of serology.

title: HIV-1–Associated Opportunistic Infections -- Evaluation
content: Microsporidiosis:  These microsporidia can be detected by light microscopy using stains such as Chromotrope 2R, calcofluor white and Uvitex 2B which can help differentiate between the spores of the microsporidia and the cells and debris (e.g., stool). In patients with gastrointestinal symptoms, if the stool testing is negative and the infection is suspected, small bowel biopsy must be considered. [23]

title: Strongyloidiasis -- Evaluation
content: As discussed above, the sensitivity of stool microscopy and serology is low, especially in patients with acute or chronic infections. It is useful to note that serologic tests have a high negative predictive value and are quite useful in excluding strongyloidiasis. [29] A 2019 study from the UK evaluating the performance of standard diagnostic techniques for this condition in patients with microscopically proven infection reported that patients with proven infection had lower serology values than those who were diagnosed based on serology and eosinophilia alone, suggesting a correlation between immunologic control of strongyloidiasis and the amplitude of the humoral response. [29] Hyperinfection can be diagnosed by studying stool, body fluids, and/or tissue with adequate sensitivity because, in this state, the host generally has a high burden of readily detectable parasites.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1799
content: Gastritis and duodenitis is a gastrointestinal inflammatory condition that is classified as a specific manifestation within the broader category of الْتِهابُ المَعِدَة (gastritis). It can give rise to or be related to الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ, indicating a more severe or erosive form of gastric inflammation. Clinically, gastritis and duodenitis has been associated with تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى, suggesting the presence of bloody stool and possible lower gastrointestinal bleeding, and with فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي, indicating that patients who are HIV‑positive may be at increased risk for or present with this condition. Recognizing these relationships assists clinicians in evaluating disease severity, considering underlying immunocompromise, and selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1828
content: The conditions نوبة (seizure), خلل الحركة المتأخر (late‑onset movement disorder), فطار غزوي, intestinal infection, and إجهاض تلقائي غير كامل (incomplete spontaneous abortion) are all recognized as entities that can be qualified as chemically induced. Identifying a chemical trigger as a possible cause is important for the evaluation and management of these diverse clinical presentations, because exposure to certain substances may precipitate seizures, movement abnormalities, gastrointestinal infections, and incomplete abortions. Awareness of the chemically induced nature of these conditions helps clinicians consider appropriate diagnostic testing, preventive strategies, and targeted therapies.

title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 51
content: embolic and thrombotic disorders to unspecified thromboembolic morphology (انصمام خثاري غير محدد). Gastritis and duodenitis, described as inflammatory forms of the stomach and duodenum (الْتِهابُ المَعِدَة, اضطراب معدي), can progress to peptic ulcer disease (الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ). Clinical observations associate gastritis and duodenitis with a positive HIV test (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي) and with hemorrhagic ulceration (تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى). Hemorrhagic infiltration (تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى) is further linked to anemia due to blood loss (فقر الدم الناتج عن فقدان الدم). These interrelated drug classifications, therapeutic indications, contraindications, and disease associations underscore the importance of careful medication selection, awareness of thrombotic risk, and comprehensive assessment of gastrointestinal inflammation, HIV status, and bleeding complications in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2145
content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with gastritis and duodenitis, indicating that the presence of this symptom complex may suggest underlying inflammation of the stomach and duodenum. Gastritis and duodenitis themselves are clinically associated with a positive HIV test (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي), highlighting a potential link between these gastrointestinal conditions and HIV infection that clinicians should consider when evaluating patients. In addition, gastritis and duodenitis have a child condition identified as الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ, suggesting that the inflammatory process may extend to or give rise to this specific gastric pathology. Recognizing these interconnected relationships can aid healthcare providers in comprehensive assessment, appropriate testing for HIV, and targeted management of related gastrointestinal disorders.

title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 952
content: Gastritis and duodenitis, a combined inflammatory condition of the stomach and duodenum, are clinically associated with the presence of تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى (bloody diarrhea) and with a فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي (positive HIV test). In addition, gastritis and duodenitis are considered a parent condition to الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ (inflammatory bowel disease), indicating that patients with these upper‑gastrointestinal inflammations may also exhibit lower‑gastrointestinal symptoms such as bloody stools, may have underlying HIV infection, and may be at risk for or progress to inflammatory bowel disease. Recognizing these interconnections can guide clinicians in comprehensive assessment and management of affected patients.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2668
content: Gastritis and duodenitis is recognized as a specific form of gastritis, with gastritis listed as its parent condition. Clinically, gastritis and duodenitis is associated with a positive HIV test (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي), indicating that patients presenting with this gastrointestinal inflammation may have a higher likelihood of HIV infection. Additionally, gastritis and duodenitis can give rise to a more severe ulcerative disorder, identified as التهاب المعدة التآكلي, reflecting a potential progression from inflammatory disease to ulcer formation. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians consider HIV screening in patients with gastritis‑duodenitis and monitor for possible development of ulcerative complications.

title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1085
content: SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض (leukopenia) and with gastritis and duodenitis. Both قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض and gastritis and duodenitis are also clinically associated with فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي (a positive HIV test). In addition, gastritis and duodenitis has a child entity, الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ (peptic ulcer disease), indicating that peptic ulcer disease is a subcategory of the upper‑gastrointestinal inflammatory process. Together, these relationships suggest that in the context of HIV infection, a symptom complex may include leukopenia, gastritis, duodenitis, and related peptic ulcer disease, highlighting the interconnected nature of hematologic and gastrointestinal manifestations in affected patients.

title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1410
content: Noninflammatory disorder of the female genital organs refers to a group of gynecologic conditions that are not caused by infection or inflammation. Within this category, two notable manifestations are كيسة رحمية (uterine cyst) and نزيف مهبلي (vaginal bleeding). Both entities are considered clinical outcomes or presentations that can arise from noninflammatory pathology of the female reproductive system, and recognizing them as part of the same disorder spectrum aids clinicians in diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected patients.

title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 985
content: The clinical entity labeled SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with findings on a chest X‑ray (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر) and with gastrointestinal inflammation described as gastritis and duodenitis. Gastritis and duodenitis themselves are clinically associated with hemorrhagic gastritis (تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى) and with a positive HIV test (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي), indicating that immunocompromised patients may present with these inflammatory conditions. Hemorrhagic gastritis is further linked to anemia resulting from blood loss (فقر الدم الناتج عن فقدان الدم), reflecting the risk of significant gastrointestinal bleeding. In addition, gastritis and duodenitis have a child condition identified as peptic ulcer disease (الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ), illustrating the progression from mucosal inflammation to ulcer formation. Together, these interconnections demonstrate how a complex of signs and symptoms can relate to radiographic chest findings, HIV status, bleeding complications, and the development of peptic ulcer disease, emphasizing the need for comprehensive evaluation in patient care.
